heading: A.1 Introduction & Procedural History
guidance: Identify the appellant (name, A-number, nationality), the Immigration Court and IJ, the decision appealed and its date, the relief denied (asylum INA 208 / withholding INA 241(b)(3) / CAT), the timely Notice of Appeal (EOIR-26, 30-day deadline under 8 CFR 1003.38), and a roadmap of the brief. Strictly from the record.

heading: A.2 Jurisdiction & Standards of Review
guidance: BIA appellate jurisdiction (8 CFR 1003.1(b), 1003.38). Standards of review under 8 CFR 1003.1(d)(3): findings of fact and credibility — including predictive findings (Matter of Z-Z-O-, 26 I&N Dec. 586 (BIA 2015)) — reviewed for CLEAR ERROR ('definite and firm conviction that a mistake has been committed'); questions of law, discretion and judgment reviewed DE NOVO. Announce, issue by issue, which standard governs each challenge this brief raises. Doctrinal only.

heading: A.3 Statement of Facts (From the Record)
guidance: The facts as they appear in the asylum filing as submitted and the hearing record reflected in the IJ's decision: protected ground(s), persecution narrative, corroborating exhibits already in the record, procedural milestones. Faithful to the record — NO new facts, no enhancement. Cite the record (application, declaration, exhibit) for each key fact where possible.

heading: A.4 The IJ's Decision: Grounds and Rationale
guidance: Enumerate EVERY dispositive ground of the decision, with fidelity and page/paragraph references where the record allows (e.g., adverse credibility, nexus, PSG cognizability, state protection, internal relocation, one-year bar, third-country/firm-resettlement, CAT denial). State the IJ's actual reasoning for each — no straw men, no omissions. This list drives the coverage of Arguments I-V: every ground listed here must be challenged somewhere.

heading: A.5 Summary of Argument
guidance: Tight map of every refutation to come: for each ground, one paragraph stating the error (legal or factual), the governing standard of review, and why reversal or remand follows. Must correspond 1:1 with Arguments I-V as developed. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.6 Argument I — Legal Errors Reviewed De Novo
guidance: Errors of law in the decision: wrong legal standard stated or applied, misconstruction of the refugee definition, PSG framework misapplied (Acosta/M-E-V-G- elements), nexus standard misstated ('one central reason'), burden-shifting errors after past persecution (8 CFR 208.13(b)(1)), failure to apply controlling precedent. For each: what the IJ said, what the law requires, why de novo review compels correction. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.7 Argument II — Clearly Erroneous Factual & Credibility Findings
guidance: Challenge fact/credibility findings under CLEAR ERROR: show the specific record passages that make each finding insupportable — not a request to re-weigh evidence. REAL ID totality framework (INA 208(b)(1)(B)(iii)): address each inconsistency the IJ relied on (explained on the record? trivial? mischaracterized?), demeanor findings contradicted by the transcript, plausibility findings contrary to country conditions in the record. Include predictive findings (Z-Z-O-). STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.8 Argument III — Record Evidence the IJ Overlooked or Misweighed
guidance: Map the evidence ALREADY IN the asylum record that the decision failed to address or mischaracterized (use the client's questionnaire answers identifying overlooked exhibits). The agency must give reasoned consideration to potentially dispositive evidence and may not ignore arguments raised (Cole v. Holder, 659 F.3d 762 (9th Cir. 2011); Sagaydak v. Gonzales, 405 F.3d 1035 (9th Cir. 2005)). For each item: what it proves, where it sits in the record, how the decision treated it (or failed to), and why that omission infects the finding it should have informed. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.9 Argument IV — CAT: Failure to Properly and Separately Adjudicate
guidance: CAT is independent, mandatory and not subject to the asylum bars: more-likely-than-not torture by or with the acquiescence (including willful blindness) of a public official (8 CFR 1208.16(c)-1208.18). Attack the specific CAT defects in this decision: nexus wrongly required; acquiescence/willful-blindness ignored; government WILLINGNESS conflated with ABILITY (Madrigal v. Holder, 716 F.3d 499 (9th Cir. 2013)); risk assessed source-by-source instead of in the AGGREGATE (Cole v. Holder); CAT not adjudicated at all despite being sought. Apply the correct standard of review per defect. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section. Also expressly challenge the withholding-of-removal denial (INA 241(b)(3) clear-probability standard) in this section so NO denied form of relief goes unchallenged.

heading: A.10 Argument V — Third-Country Grounds Do Not Bar Relief (Conditional)
guidance: ONLY if the IJ's decision relied on a third-country ground. If it did NOT: output ONE sentence stating that the decision rests on no third-country ground and this section is inapplicable — nothing else. If it did: rebut with precision — firm resettlement framework (INA 208(b)(2)(A)(vi); Matter of A-G-G-, 25 I&N Dec. 486 (BIA 2011): DHS bears the initial prima facie burden of showing an offer of permanent resettlement); transit-presumption rules (Circumvention of Lawful Pathways) are rebuttable, carry exceptions, and remain in active litigation — address their current status accurately; withholding and CAT are NOT subject to these bars. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.11 Motion to Remand — New Evidence (Conditional)
guidance: ONLY if new supporting evidence was uploaded for this appeal. If NONE: output ONE sentence noting no new evidence accompanies this appeal — nothing else. If there is: frame as a motion to remand under 8 CFR 1003.2(c) (motions to remand follow the substantive requirements of motions to reopen, Matter of Coelho, 20 I&N Dec. 464 (BIA 1992)). For EACH new item (identified by name): (1) what it is and what specific point of the asylum claim it reinforces — exactly as the client tied it in the questionnaire; (2) MATERIALITY — why it could change the outcome on a dispositive ground; (3) UNAVAILABILITY — the client's honest explanation of why it was not available and could not have been discovered or presented at the former hearing (cost, impossibility of obtaining it from the home country, unawareness, post-hearing date). Use ONLY the client's stated justification — never fabricate one. New evidence must never be argued as if it were part of the hearing record. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.12 Controlling & Persuasive Precedent Applied
guidance: For each verified precedent from the research bundle: court, citation, holding, and a DIRECT factual analogy to this appellant's case — why it compels sustaining the appeal, reversal or remand on a specific ground. Prefer authorities matching the IJ's error types (credibility, failure to consider evidence, CAT acquiescence/aggregation, standard of review). Distinguish the adverse authorities the IJ relied on. Verified citations only; include verified URLs inline only. STRUCTURAL DISCIPLINE: use EXACTLY the heading provided for this section; refer to other parts of the brief ONLY by their A.x numbers and never invent an internal "Argument N" numbering different from these headings; complete every sentence and paragraph before the section ends and NEVER spill a heading, announcement or opening line of another argument into this section.

heading: A.13 Conclusion & Prayer for Relief
guidance: Synthesize the errors (legal, factual, evidentiary, CAT, third-country if applicable) in tight recap paragraphs; firm final position. Formal Prayer: sustain the appeal; vacate/reverse the decision; grant asylum (or withholding/CAT as applicable); in the alternative, remand for further proceedings consistent with the arguments — and, if a11 applies, remand for consideration of the new evidence.
